fact: July 8 is exactly 189 days into the year, which means there are only 176 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is typically prime time for fireflies to put on their most dazzling light shows - they're basically nature's disco balls!
Mosquitoes often seem extra active around July 8 because summer heat makes them breed rapidly, and they can smell you from 100 feet away!
July 8 falls in the middle of Cancer season in the zodiac calendar, running from late June through most of July.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere countries, July 8 is deep winter, which means penguins are huddling together for warmth while the north experiences summer heat!
By July 8, sunrise is getting noticeably later each day in most northern places - summer daylight is already starting its slow retreat!
Cicadas are typically at their noisiest around this time of year, with their buzzing reaching up to 120 decibels - that's louder than a chainsaw!
July 8 is usually prime cherry season in many temperate regions, and a single cherry tree can produce up to 7,000 cherries!
Ocean temperatures in the Northern Hemisphere are still climbing toward their yearly peak around July 8, which typically won't happen until late August.
Bats are often extra active around July 8 because insects are everywhere - a single bat can gobble up 1,000 mosquitoes in just one hour!
In northern regions like Alaska, July 8 still typically has nearly 20 hours of daylight, which means kids there can play outside until almost midnight!
Corn plants are usually growing so fast around this date that you can literally hear them creaking and popping as they stretch toward the sky - it sounds like nature's Rice Krispies!
